metaDescription: Understand the difference between exempt and non-exempt employees under California wage laws, including overtime, breaks, and misclassification issues.
introText: California wage law does not classify employees by title alone. The real question is whether the employee satisfies the salary and duties requirements for exemption. When an employer gets that wrong, the result can be years of unpaid overtime, missed-break premiums, waiting-time penalties, and inaccurate wage statements.

comparisons.body: Usually entitled to overtime, meal and rest breaks, minimum-wage protections, and detailed wage-statement compliance. Many hourly and frontline employees fall into this category.
Usually not entitled to overtime or break premiums, but only if the employer proves the salary basis and duties tests are genuinely met under California law.

comparisonTable.left: Generally eligible for daily and weekly overtime protections.
comparisonTable.right: Usually not eligible, but only if the exemption is valid.

comparisonTable.left: Protected by California meal and rest break rules.
comparisonTable.right: Break-premium rules generally do not apply the same way.

comparisonTable.left: Default in many workplaces unless the employer proves exemption.
comparisonTable.right: Requires salary and duties tests, not just a management-sounding title.

comparisonTable.left: Working off the clock or unpaid overtime.
comparisonTable.right: Being labeled exempt even though the day-to-day work is mostly routine, production, or frontline work.

sections.header: Why title alone is not enough
sections.body: California looks at what the employee actually does. An employee may be called a manager, supervisor, coordinator, or specialist and still be non-exempt if most time is spent performing ordinary operational work instead of true executive, administrative, or professional duties.

sections.header: Why misclassification cases can become valuable
sections.body: Misclassification claims often stack multiple wage-law violations together. Unpaid overtime, missed meal periods, missed rest periods, waiting-time penalties, wage-statement penalties, and interest may all be part of the same case if the employee was treated as exempt when California law says otherwise.

Look at how your day is actually spent, whether you direct other employees, how often you exercise independent judgment, how you are paid, and whether you regularly work more than eight hours in a day or forty in a week without overtime. Those facts matter more than the employer's label.

decisionGuide.useCase: You are salaried but spend most of the day doing routine operational work
decisionGuide.reason: Salary alone does not make someone exempt under California law.

decisionGuide.useCase: You supervise others and make meaningful policy or personnel decisions
decisionGuide.reason: Real executive authority and qualifying duties matter.

decisionGuide.useCase: You frequently work long days with no overtime despite limited authority
decisionGuide.reason: That fact pattern often appears in strong wage-and-hour claims.

bottomLine: The practical question is not whether the employer called you exempt. It is whether California law agrees. When duties and pay structure do not match the label, the employee may be owed substantial unpaid compensation.

faq.question: Am I exempt just because I have a manager title?
faq.answer: No. Your real duties, discretion, and salary structure matter far more than your title.

faq.question: What can I recover if I was misclassified?
faq.answer: Potential recovery may include unpaid overtime, missed-break premiums, waiting-time penalties, wage-statement penalties, interest, and attorney fees depending on the facts.

contactUsText: Contact The Azizi Firm for a wage-and-hour review if your employer classified you as exempt but your day-to-day job duties do not match that label.
